Over that past 10 years, on-surface chemistry has become an intensively investigated research area. In contrast to classical solution-phase chemistry, bond-forming processes occur on a metal substrate under ultra-high vacuum (UHV) without any solvent assistance and the success of these reactions is followed by scanning tunneling microscopy (STM) analysis. Notably, bottom-up on-surface synthesis allows structures to be accessed that cannot be prepared via solution-phase chemistry due to solubility issues. Reactions that have been investigated include Ullmann coupling, (1-7) imine formation, (8-10) dehydration of boronic acids, (11) dimerization of N-heterocyclic carbenes, (12) imide formation, (13) polyester formation, (14) cycloadditions, (15-17) dehydrogenations, (18-22) and decarboxylative coupling, (23) among others. (24-31)
Most processes deal with either intramolecular reactions (32, 33) or with intermolecular self-reactions in which a single type of reacting organic moiety is involved. This is not unexpected because intermolecular on-surface chemistry between two different organic compounds is highly challenging. This is due to the fact that two organic compounds have to be co-adsorbed and self-assembled on the substrate ideally in a mixed self-assembly phase. If no mixed phase is generated, disassembly and mixing of the two separated phases have to be achieved during annealing, which should subsequently lead to bond-forming events. For this to occur, the physical properties of the two different compounds with respect to their reactivities (no self-reaction) and desorption behaviors have to be well-adjusted. In fact, many examples of successful intermolecular on-surface reactions between two different organic compounds carrying the same reacting moiety have been disclosed, (29-31) but only a few cases have been reported to date for systems carrying different moieties, (8, 9, 13, 15, 27, 28) as shown Scheme 1a–c. Along these lines, the azide/alkyne click reaction was successfully conducted on Cu(111). (15) Imide bond formation (13) and aldehyde/amine condensation to give imines (8, 9, 27, 28) were achieved on Au(111)/Ag(111) substrates. Condensation of boronic acids to give boroxines (11) and trapping of isocyanates with amines to generate ureas (34) were found to occur on noble metal substrates.
These transformations comprise either addition reactions where no byproduct is generated or condensation reactions where the byproduct (water) is readily removed by desorption under UHV conditions. An intermolecular reaction comprising σ-bond metathesis is currently unknown (Scheme 1d). Herein, we disclose the intermolecular reaction of alkynyl silanes with carboxylic acids on Ag(111) and Au(111) to give terminal alkynes along with silyl esters via σ-bond metathesis (proton–silyl group exchange), which is different from the reported thermal removal of protecting groups (35) and should be highly valuable in both silyl protecting group (36) and on-surface covalent coupling (37, 38) chemistry. We show that this reaction is valuable for the self-assembly of inherently unstable organic alkynes via a strategy that comprises on-surface silyl group exchange and self-assembly of the in situ generated terminal alkynes. It is also important to show that such σ-bond metathesis can be combined with Glaser coupling to prepare polymers on-surface via a cascade process.
The synthesis of compounds 1, 3, 5, and 7 is described in the Chemical Synthesis section of the Supporting Information (structures of the compounds are shown in Scheme 2).
All experiments were conducted using an Omicron LT-STM system (78 K) with a base pressure of 1 × 10–10 mbar. Imaging was performed in constant current mode, with the bias voltage given with respect to the sample. Metal surfaces (Au(111), Ag(111), and Cu(111)) were cleaned by several cycles of Ar+ sputtering and annealing. The HOPG surface was freshly cleaved with duct tape and then transferred to the UHV chamber for measurements. Molecules were evaporated from a quartz crucible onto metal/semimetal surfaces, and the sublimation temperatures for molecule 1, 3, 5, 7, and NDCA were 90, 160, 120, 70, and 190 °C, respectively. The deposition rate was calibrated by STM imaging to ∼0.2 mL/min (monolayer per minute). Room-temperature (RT) annealing of the sample was conducted in the carousel position, whereas thermal annealing was conducted in the sample storage position of the manipulator and monitored by an IR detector. Within this work, thermal annealing above RT was usually conducted for 30 min. The SPECS X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) system was directly connected to the LT-STM, allowing samples to be transferred without breaking the vacuum. XPS spectroscopy was performed on the selected σ-bond metathesis system in the UHV chamber.
DFT calculations comprise two parts: (i) DFT calculations on the reaction mechanism of a reduced model system at the Au(111), Ag(111) and Cu(111) surfaces, consisting of two molecules with one alkynylsilyl and one carboxylic acid group, and (ii) DFT calculation on the geometries, bond energies, and HOMO–LUMO gaps of molecule (4)n. For details, see the DFT Methods section of the Supporting Information.
Initial experiments were conducted with 2,6-naphthalenedicarboxylic acid (NDCA) and 4,9-bis((trimethylsilyl)ethynyl)anthracene (1) (Figure 1). NDCA and 1 were successfully co-deposited on Ag(111) (for details, see the Supporting Information). Figure 1a shows the submolecular STM image of 1 and NDCA after deposition onto the Ag(111) surface. It is obvious from the image that the two organic molecules do not mix upon adsorption and self-assemble in two separated pure phases. Anthracene 1 is imaged as a dumbbell, in which the TMS groups appear as bright spots (Figure 1a, inset). NDCA was previously self-assembled on Ag(111), (23) and for the NDCA phase, the same images were recorded. Only a few molecules of 1 were attached to the island edges of NDCA (Figure S1).
Upon annealing the 1 and NDCA-covered Ag(111) substrate at RT for 64 h, we surprisingly observed σ-bond metathesis (silyl group exchange) between anthracene 1 and NDCA to give desilylated anthracene 2, Si-NDCA, and Si2-NDCA. As for 1, the trimethylsilyl groups in silyl esters Si-NDCA and Si2-NDCA appear as bright spots in the images (Figure 1b). Desilylated anthracene 2 formed molecular 1D chains at the surface (Figure 1c). STM manipulations on such chains proved that the molecules of 2 are linked by van der Waals interactions and not by covalent bonding (Figure S2).
Unfortunately, intermediate structures supporting a potential mechanism for the silyl exchange could not be identified. The formation of Ag-carboxylates is unlikely because in such a case the H2 formed as a byproduct would desorb, thereby removing the proton source. In addition, Ag-carboxylates were not identified as intermediates, and metal carboxylate formation at the substrate generally requires higher temperatures, whereas in our experiments thermal annealing at Ag did not increase the reaction efficiency. (23) Therefore, it is deduced that these reactions occur via σ-bond metathesis. Because transformations did not proceed at RT on Au(111) and proceeded with very low efficiency on Cu(111) (Figure S3), the Ag substrate is involved in the process, likely by interacting with the triple bond. Furthermore, we found that this on-surface σ-bond metathesis does not occur on a HOPG surface, indicating the importance of the metal substrate in the reaction (Figure S4). Additionally, azelaic acid does not work as well as NDCA does for this σ-bond metathesis.
To strengthen our hypothesis and obtain additional insight into the reaction mechanism, we performed DFT calculations. A reduced model system, consisting of two molecules with one alkynylsilyl and one carboxylic acid group (Figure 2), was used to reduce the structural complexity, enabling an efficient description of the reaction mechanism. First, the reaction was considered in the gas phase to elucidate the intrinsic properties of the transition state for the σ-bond metathesis. As shown in Figure 2a, the alkyne is bent and a concerted exchange of the silyl group and proton occurs. However, as this structural deformation is energetically unfavorable in the gas phase, the reaction is hindered by a high transition state energy (E = 1.82 eV). In contrast, the adsorption of trimethylsilylalkynyl benzene on the Ag surface causes, due to the interaction of the metal surface with the triple bond and the steric repulsion of the bulky silyl group, a distortion (bending) of the triple bond (Figure 2b). As a result, the triple bond is activated and can then act as a proton acceptor for the metathesis reaction. In addition to this preorganization of the molecule on the surface, the carbonyl oxygen atom of the carboxylic acid functionality, which is not involved in the reaction, and the proton, which is transferred, interact with the Ag surface (Figure 2c). Consequently, the transition state energy for the concerted reaction is reduced with respect to that of the gas-phase reaction by 0.56 eV, and in line with the STM measurements, the exchange reaction takes place. For the Au(111) and Cu(111) surfaces, comparable decreases in the reaction barrier for the σ-bond metathesis were calculated: 0.53 and 0.65 eV, respectively.
Our DFT calculations highlight the catalytic role of the metal surface for the σ-bond metathesis by its interaction with both the triple bond and the acid group (Figure 2). To further quantitatively discriminate the reactivity of the different metal surfaces, kinetic processes such as on-surface diffusion have to be taken into account, which is beyond the scope of the current study. However, at this point, it can be assumed that strong molecule–surface interactions reduce the molecular diffusion ability on the Cu surface, which results in a rather low overall efficiency of the σ-bond metathesis on this surface.
To investigate the generality of the silyl exchange, we studied two additional silylated alkynes. Pleasingly, the on-surface silyl transfer on Ag(111) to NDCA also worked with 6,13-bis((trimethylsilyl)ethynyl)pentacene (5) to give 6,13-bis(ethynyl)pentacene (6), albeit at higher temperature (155 °C; see Figures S5–S7). Similarly, such σ-bond metathesis on Ag(111) at RT also worked with persilylated hexaethynylbenzene 3, which was first synthesized by Vollhardt and co-workers. (39) It is well-known that hexaethynylbenzene 4 has limited stability in solution and is unstable in the solid state. (39) After co-adsorption of 3 and NDCA, clear phase separation between the organic compounds occurred, and for both components, regular self-assembly structures were obtained (Figure 3a). Only a few molecules of 3 were identified at the edges of the NDCA islands. In the overview image of these two precursors, the islands consisting of silylated alkyne 3 are higher in contrast than the NDCA islands because of the bulky TMS protecting groups.
After RT annealing (up to 1 h), we noticed that the silyl exchange reaction started to proceed (Figure S8). Upon prolonging the annealing to around 17.5 h, the σ-bond metathesis reaction occurred at the Ag(111) surface to a large extent, and products 4, Si-NDCA, and Si2-NDCA appeared in separated self-assembly phases, as shown in Figure 3b. We also identified a small area consisting of a mixture of Si-NDCA and Si2-NDCA. It is not unexpected that these two silyl esters with similar structures form a mixed self-assembly phase. High-resolution STM images of each reaction product, Si-NDCA (Figure 3b-ii), Si2-NDCA (Figure 3b-iii), and 4 (Figure 3b-iv), were recorded. Interestingly, we found that hexaethynylbenzene 4 can desorb from the Ag(111) surface because we noted that upon further extending the RT annealing (up to 63 h) a significantly lower amount of 4 remained at the Ag substrate. This strongly supports the alkynes of 4 being proton-terminated after σ-bond metathesis.
The effect of the feed ratio 3 with respect to NDCA was also investigated. It was found that increasing the amount of NDCA led to a larger fraction of monosilyl ester Si-NDCA, as expected from stoichiometry. This also indicates that the reactivity of the acid functionalities in NDCA is similar to the reactivity of the acid functionality in the mono silyl ester Si-NDCA (no activating effect by the silyl group). Along these lines, increasing the stoichiometry of 3 provided mainly 4 and bissilyl ester Si2-NDCA as major products. As for the other two silylated alkynes investigated, σ-bond metathesis of NDCA and 3 did not work on Au(111) and worked with a very low efficiency on the Cu(111) surface.
It is important to note that all of our attempts to generate clean self-assembly structures of 4 on noble metal substrates by solution-phase silyl deprotection of 3, isolation, and subsequent UHV adsorption of 4 on various substrates failed, (40) clearly documenting the value of our novel “silyl exchange approach” for the assembly of unstable alkynes. Electronic properties of well-ordered hexaethynylbenzene monolayers were studied (Figure S9). It was found that individual hexaethynylbenzenes weakly interact electronically, likely via interaction of their alkynyl substituents. This has been further supported by STM tip manipulation experiments (Figure S10) and DFT calculations (Figures S11 and S12 and Tables S1 and S2). Additionally, a high-resolution STM image of 4 showed clearly that each alkynyl substituent can be distinguished and also showed the packing of these subunits in the self-assembly (Figure 3c). The periodic unit parameters of the self-assembly structure of molecule 4 were measured as a = 1.01 ± 0.01 nm, b = 1.01 ± 0.01 nm, and θ = 60.0 ± 0.2°, which match very well with DFT calculations (Figure S12) and clearly indicate that the alkyne groups are proton-terminated after σ-bond metathesis.
To further analyze the proton–silyl group exchange on Ag(111), additional core-level XPS experiments were carried out on the NDCA and molecule 3 system. To avoid the reaction partially occurring during the XPS measurements, the data referring to the starting point of the reaction were acquired by two individual experiments with the exclusive deposition of NDCA and molecule 3, respectively. However, due to the mixture of molecule precursors as well as the presence of product and byproducts after σ-bond metathesis, the XPS C 1s spectra alone do not provide unambiguous final proof of the σ-bond metathesis (Figure S14). In contrast, the Si 2p and O 1s spectra provide very clean signals, indicating the σ-bond metathesis by XPS measurements.
Figure 4a shows XPS data corresponding to the Si 2p region for compound 3 alone (top) and the mixture of molecule 3 and NDCA (bottom) measured after σ-bond metathesis (RT annealing > 60 h). As the Si 2p region is located on the high binding energy slope of the Ag 4s peak, the intensity of the Ag 4s line has to be included during the peak fitting of the Si 2p line (see Figure S13). Examination of the Si 2p spectrum reveals an asymmetric peak shape due to the 2p3/2–1/2 spin–orbit splitting. The peak fits of the Si 2p doublet were accomplished by two components with a branching ratio of 0.5 and a spin–orbit splitting of 0.63 eV. The analysis of molecule 3 (top in Figure 4a) revealed the Si 2p3/2 peak at Eb = 100.97 eV (Si 2p1/2Eb = 101.60 eV). This is in good agreement with values reported for silicon atoms bound only to carbon. Figure 4a shows the XPS data and peak fitting analysis for the Si 2p photoemission signals of the mixture of molecule 3 and NDCA (bottom) after σ-bond metathesis. The photoemission intensity of the Si 2p3/2 peak appears significantly shifted at Eb = 101.61 eV (Si 2p1/2Eb= 102.24 eV). This position can be attributed to Si–O–C bond formation, as reported, (41-45) indicating bond cleavage between the ethynyl moiety and the silyl group as well as the subsequent formation of a Si–O bond between the protecting group and NCDA.
The O 1s data are depicted in Figure 4b. The experimental data for the measurement of only the NDCA molecules (top) are well described by two components with a stoichiometric ratio of 1:1, representing hydroxyl (Eb = 533.8 eV, green) and carbonyl (Eb = 532.5 eV, blue) oxygens. (46-49) The data of the mixture of molecule 3 and NDCA after σ-bond metathesis are shown in the bottom of Figure 4b. It is obvious that the peak at higher binding energy is now absent and the peak corresponding to the carbonyl oxygen appears slightly shifted to Eb = 532.7 eV. At lower binding energy, a new oxygen species is visible at Eb = 531.3 eV, which is in agreement with data reported for oxygen connected to a comparable silyl protecting group. (50) Therefore, we ascribe this peak to oxygen atoms in Si–O–C bonds after the σ-bond metathesis. Both peaks appear slightly more broadened compared to the NDCA measurement, which is likely caused by a more complex chemical environment in the local adsorbate geometry in the case of silylated NDCA molecules.
To explore potential applications of the σ-bond metathesis in on-surface protecting group chemistry, we included 1,4-bis((trimethylsilyl)-ethynyl)-2,5-dihexylbenzene (7) in these studies. In the absence of NDCA, compound 7 at the Ag surface after thermal annealing did not engage in any defined reaction but underwent desorption or formed unidentified species (Figure S15). However, 7 coadsorbed with NDCA should undergo desilylation via σ-bond metathesis to form 1,4-bis(ethynyl)-2,5-dihexylbenzene (8) along with silyl esters Si-NDCA and Si2-NDCA. Alkyne 8, in turn, might then undergo on-surface Glaser coupling to give covalent polymers, as reported in our previous work. (20, 21)
Indeed, it was found that after co-adsorption of 7 and NDCA onto the Ag(111) surface, clear phase separation between the organic compounds occurred, and for both components, regular self-assembly structures were obtained (Figure 5b-i). Only a few molecules of 7 were identified at the edges of the NDCA islands (Figure 5b-ii), and a high-resolution STM image of molecule 7 is provided in Figure S15. After RT annealing for 64 h, the σ-bond metathesis reaction occurred to a large extent at the Ag(111) surface, resulting in the formation of products 8, Si-NDCA, and Si2-NDCA, which all appeared in separated self-assembly phases, as shown in the overview STM image in Figure 5c-i. A small area consisting of a mixture of Si-NDCA and Si2-NDCA was also identified. High-resolution STM images of each reaction product were recorded (8, Figure 5c-ii; Si2-NDCA, Figure 5c-iii; and Si-NDCA: Figure 5c-iv,v). It is important to note that the STM image of 8 in situ generated at the surface via desilylation agrees very well with our previously reported image for the same compound adsorbed on Ag(111). (20) A detailed comparison of STM images at different voltages for in situ and presynthesized 8 is given in Figure S16. All of these results strongly support the occurrence of σ-bond metathesis at the surface. It is also clear that after σ-bond metathesis the alkynes are terminated by protons and the acids are terminated by silyls, which are different from reported deprotonated alkynes/acids. (51-53) Interestingly, we also found a new phase of Si-NDCA (Figure 5c-v), where the bulky TMS protecting groups lie in zigzag lines rather than in straight lines, as shown in Figure 5c-iv.
After thermal annealing up to 120 °C, small islands of mixed products between Glaser polymers/oligomers, Si-NDCA, and Si2-NDCA were obtained, as shown Figure 5d-i. The Glaser coupling polymers of product 8 are clearly identified, as shown in Figure 5d-ii,iii. (20) However, the coupling efficiency was low, Glaser polymers formed under these conditions are rather short, and the polymer product phase did not separate from the byproducts.
To increase the length of the Glaser polymers and to realize the separation of the Glaser polymers from Si-NDCA and Si2-NDCA, σ-bond metathesis followed by coupling was also tested on Au(111) and Cu(111). The Cu(111) surface showed low reactivity for both the σ-bond metathesis and the Glaser coupling. This is likely due to strong molecule–Cu substrate interactions, leading to a low molecular diffusion ability of the individual reaction partners, which is a prerequisite for efficient on-surface chemistry. However, Au(111) was found to be suitable for the cascade reaction comprising a σ-bond metathesis and subsequent Glaser coupling at 170 °C (high-temperature annealing above 120 °C was necessary to trigger the σ-bond metathesis on Au(111)). As shown in Figure 6a, Glaser polymers are clearly formed and the Si2-NDCA byproduct generated is visible. Importantly, the polymers and Si2-NDCA appear in separated phases. High-resolution STM images on the Glaser polymers and byproduct Si2-NDCA are shown Figure 6b,c.
In summary, by discovering the on-surface silyl exchange reaction (σ-bond metathesis), we have introduced a new strategy for the self-assembly of alkynes that are inherently unstable in solution and in the solid state. σ-bond metathesis was confirmed by STM and XPS analyses. We found that σ-bond metathesis works efficiently at the Ag(111) surface at RT, whereas the same transformation on Au, HOPG, and Cu surfaces was not efficient. However, upon thermal annealing, σ-bond metathesis also occurs at the Au(111) surface. Silyl exchange is general, as shown for four different silylated alkynes. DFT studies provide insights into the reaction mechanism. To document the potential, we applied the strategy to the generation of a highly ordered self-assembly structure of hexaethynylbenzene, an organic molecule that is unstable in solution and in the solid state. A monolayer of hexaethynylbenzene cannot be generated by using the traditional “protecting group free” approach due to the high instability of this compound. Furthermore, such σ-bond metathesis can also be combined with Glaser coupling in a cascade reaction to fabricate covalent polymers at surfaces. The cascade reaction works most efficiently at the Au(111) surface upon thermal annealing. Notably, silyl protection/deprotection is a well-established strategy in solution-phase chemistry, which we believe also has great potential in the area of on-surface chemistry.
